name: Ilya Prigogine
core_thesis: Article-backed thinker stub created during primitives_v2 Phase 2 from `data/articles/source/prigogine.md`.

cross_engagements.brief: Abhinavagupta presses the same substrate structure axis in the metaphysical register, but with the value `one-self-standing` rather than `process-field-with-no-enduring-substrate`.
Adi Sankara presses the same manifestation status axis in the metaphysical register, but with the value `sublatable-not-null` rather than `dependent-real`.
Gaudapada (Samkhya commentator) presses the same temporal mode axis in the metaphysical register, but with the value `substance-primary` rather than `process-primary`.
Alfred North Whitehead presses the same evolution register axis in the historical-genealogical register, but with the value `durational-creative-growth` rather than `real-cosmological-evolution`.
Akalanka presses the same soteric end axis in the soteriological register, but with the value `isolation-or-discriminative-release` rather than `not-soteriological`.
Baladeva Vidyabhushana presses the same causation model axis in the metaphysical register, but with the value `unchanged-ground-with-changing-power` rather than `processual-concrescence`.

engaged_works.title: Order Out of Chaos
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored modern monograph; bibliographic ascription is uncontroversial.
engaged_works.summary: Prigogine and Stengers' joint book on the philosophical implications of non-equilibrium thermodynamics, dissipative structures, and the irreversibility of time. The argument is that the classical-mechanical reversible-time picture is a limiting idealization, and that the actual physics of complex open systems exhibits irreversible, history-dependent, far-from-equilibrium dynamics — the "dissipative structures" for which Prigogine received the 1977 Nobel Prize. The book is read in the comparative corpus as a contemporary Western interlocutor of the Vedāntic doctrine of *parināma* / *vivarta* and of process metaphysics more generally — sharing with Whitehead the rejection of substance-snapshot ontology in favor of a process-and-event ontology, but supplied by physics rather than by metaphysical analysis. The Bergson-Prigogine dialogue (Prigogine writes admiringly of Bergson) is one of the principal twentieth-century scientific defenses of the durational-process metaphysics that the Vedānta-corpus engages.

engaged_works.title: The End of Certainty
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored modern monograph; bibliographic ascription is uncontroversial.
engaged_works.summary: Prigogine's later technical-philosophical statement on the implications of chaos theory, non-integrable dynamical systems, and Poincaré resonances for the foundations of physics. The book argues that time-irreversibility is not a phenomenological artifact but a fundamental feature of the dynamics of certain large-Poincaré-system classes, and that this finding overturns the classical-Laplacian deterministic worldview. Read in the comparative corpus as a contemporary scientific witness to the process-metaphysics tradition (Whitehead, Bergson, Aurobindo) against the substance-snapshot tradition (classical mechanics, mainstream analytic metaphysics).
